video_title: Conservation of angular momentum | AP Physics | Khan Academy

segments.text:
- [Instructor] Did you
know that a satellite
or a space telescope can turn
by any amount about any axis in space
without using any external force,
without any thrusters?
It can do that.
How?
By using a principle called conservation
of angular momentum.
But what exactly is this?
Let's find out.

Let's start with something
that we already know.
We know that if an object of mass m,
has some velocity v, it has momentum,
which is the product of mass and velocity.
Why do we care
about this thing called as momentum?
Well, that's because if you have a bunch

of particles, not just
one, but a bunch of them,

and if the total external
force on that system
of particles is 0,
then the momentum of the system,
which is basically the sum

of individual momenta,
that stays a constant.
It doesn't change.
And we call this the principle
of conservation of linear momentum.
And this is a super powerful tool
in making predictions.
For example, imagine a situation

in which a block is
coming with some velocity
and hits another block.
And let's say after the collision,

they stick together
and they move together.
We wanna find what that final velocity is.
Well, if you consider both m1, m2
as our system, and if we ignore friction

and air resistance, then
during the collision,
the forces are all internal,
there are no external forces.
Then we can say momentum
the total momentum of the system,
before the collision

should equal the total
momentum of the system
after the collision.
And just from this in a couple of steps,
we can figure out
what that final velocity should be.
Isn't this awesome?
Why does it work out this way?
Well, that's because
if there are no external forces acting
on the system, then during the collision,

when m1 puts a force
on m2, m2 puts an equal
but opposite force back on m1.
So whatever momentum has gained by m2,

the same amount of momentum
must be lost by m1.
And so the total momentum does not change.

Well, now you might
say in real life cases,
external forces are not 0.
You have friction,
you have air resistance, right?
Yeah, but if this was a situation
just before the collision
and this is the situation
just after the collision,
then during the collision,

the internal forces are
much higher compared
to the external ones.
And so we can ignore them.
We can pretty much say that hey,
momentum changes mostly happen
due to the internal forces.

And so we can pretty
much use this principle
and make pretty accurate predictions.
Okay, now guess what?
If a rigid body is spinning,
then we know it has angular momentum,
which is given as the product

of its rotational inertia
and angular velocity.
And many experiments show
for a bunch of rigid bodies,
if the net external torque on it is 0,

then the angular momentum
of the entire system,
which is again basically the sum
of an individual angular momenta,
that stays a constant.
So now we have something

called the conservation
of angular momentum.

The idea is very, very
similar to over here,

except that this will
be used for rotation.
So let's take a few examples.
Let's say we have a disc
that is spinning on a frictionless table
and let the angular velocity be omega 1.
Now let's say we drop another disc.
This is not initially rotating.
Okay, we're gonna drop it on this one.
And there is friction between these two.
So this is spinning, this is not.

What happens when you
drop this on this one?
Well, because there is friction
between these two, the big disc is gonna
put a torque on the smaller one trying
to make it spin in this direction.

But of course this small
disc will put an equal
but an opposite torque on the bigger disc,
slowing it down.

Eventually they'll have
the same angular velocity,

and when that happens,
the torque disappears,
and now they'll be both spinning together
with the same angular velocity.

And notice, if I consider
both the disc as part

of my system, there
are no external torques
and therefore, the total angular momentum
of the system must stay conserved.
So look, just with this principle,
I can now predict

what that final angular
velocity is going to be.

I can say that, hey, the
total angular momentum
of my system before,
which is just rotational inertia I1

of this disc times its
angular velocity, omega 1.
And what's the direction?
Well remember for angular momentum,
we can use our right hand thumb rule,

our four curling fingers
curl in the direction
of the angular velocity,
then the thumb points in the direction
of the angular momentum.
So over here, the angular momentum
is just upwards, okay?
Plus the angular momentum of this one,
which is just 0, I2 times 0,

that should equal the
final angular momentum,
which is going
to be the total rotational inertia,
which is just I1 plus I2
times the final angular velocity.
And look, just from this, I can calculate

what that final angular
velocity is going to be.
Isn't it amazing?
Now, of course, one key thing over here is

that the rotational
inertia not only depends
on the mass, but it also depends
upon how that mass is distributed.
If the mass is distributed farther away
from the axis of rotation,
the rotational inertia is higher.
So the distribution of mass also matters.
For example, for a disc,

the rotational inertia is
given as 1/2 mR square,
where m is the mass of the disc
and R is the radius.
So we could plug that in and simplify,

but you get the point,
so we'll not do that.
Okay, let's take another example.
Consider spinning tires of an automobile.
Imagine the car is jacked up
so the tires are not touching the ground.
So the ground is not exerting any force
or torque on these tires.
Right now, the clutch is not engaged,
so the two tires can spin independently
because they're not connected.
So let's say they're spinning
with two different angular velocities,
omega 1 and omega 2, okay?

Now what will happen
if I engage the clutch?
Well, now these two axes are connected
and they're now forced to spin
with one particular angular velocity.
So now they will have some
new angular velocity omega.
If we ignore friction,

and if we ignore the mass
of the clutch over here
and all of that, can you predict

what that final angular
velocity should be?
Assume that the rotational inertia
of this wheel is I1,
and that of this wheel is I2.
It'll be a great area to pause the video
and see if you can use the conservation
of angular momentum to predict

what that final angular
velocity should be.

Alright, we're gonna
consider the two wheels
as the part of our system.
We're gonna ignore the mass of the axles
and the clutch and everything, okay?

So what's the total
initial angular momentum?

Well, it's going to be
the angular momentum

of this wheel, which is
just I1 times omega 1,

and we have to be careful
about the direction.

In this particular case,
the angular momentum
of this wheel is going to be to the right.

And this wheel also has
the angular momentum
in the same direction, so I can add it.
So plus I2 omega 2,
that should equal the total
final angular momentum.
And the total final angular momentum
would be just I1 plus I2 times omega.
And boom!

From this I can calculate
what omega should be.
Of course you could say that, hey,
the two wheels are identical,
so I1 should be equal to I2.
And we can further simplify this,
but you get the point.

Okay, now consider a box
floating in outer space
with a spinning disc inside of it.
The disc is connected to a motor
and the motor is powered by a battery.
So everything, the disc, the motor,
and the battery, all of it is completely
inside the box.
But because of the spinning disc,
the entire box disc system
has some angular momentum, right?

But there are no external
torques on this system.
So this angular momentum of this system
must stay constant, right?
Okay, so now imagine we could remotely
control the motor and change the speed
of this spinning disc.
What do you think would happen
if we reduced its speed?
Well, the angular momentum
of the disc would decrease,
but the total angular momentum
must stay the same.
So how will nature ensure that?

The answer is that the
box itself will begin
to rotate in the same direction?
That way the decrease

in the disc's angular
momentum is compensated
by an equal increase
in the box's angular momentum,
keeping the total unchanged.
So look, simply by slowing down the disc,
we can make the entire box start rotating.
No external force or torque's needed.
Pretty cool right?
Now, what do you think will happen
if we speed the disc back up
to its original speed?
Well, now the angular momentum
of the disc returns to its original value.
So the box will stop rotating,
again to keep the total angle
of momentum the same as before.

Finally, what if we
increase the disc speed?
Well, that would try
to increase the total angular momentum.

So to compensate the
box would start rotating
in the opposite direction.

Again, ensuring the
overall angular momentum

remains the same, which
means just by increasing

or decreasing the speed
of a spinning disc,
we can rotate this box by any amount
in any direction.
This device is called a reaction wheel.
And this is how we control
rotations of satellites.
So here's an example
of how these reaction wheels look like.
We have three reaction wheels
to control rotations along three axes,
and we usually have another set
as a backup just in case some
of the others stop working.

So this is how by using
the reaction wheels,
we can rotate our satellites
or our telescopes by any amount
and along any particular axis.
Pretty awesome, right?
What's more awesome is that conservation
of angular momentum can also be applied
to non rigid bodies.
Take a look at this.

You have an ice skater
who starts out spinning
with low speed, but then look,
she starts speeding up.
Can you pause the video and think
about what's going on over here?
See if you can explain this using the idea
of the conservation of angular momentum.
Alright, let's see.

When she starts spinning,
look at how her arms
and legs are stretched out.
Because of this, a lot of particles
are far away from the axis
of rotation distributed far away
from the axis of rotation.

As a result, her rotation
inertia is pretty high.
She also has some angular velocity,

and therefore, she has
some angular momentum,
which is the product of these two.
Now, as she continues to spin, we will see

that she will pull her
arms and legs closer.
As a result, a lot of particles now

are distributed close
to the axis of rotation,

therefore, her rotational
inertia goes down.
But what happens to her angular momentum?
It has to stay the same.
Why?
Well, because all the pooling forces

were internal, so all
those torques are internal,

and there are no external
torques over here.

I mean, of course there
is force of friction,
but that is very minimal.
So the torque produced by the friction
is very minimal.

So if I consider this
ice skater as our system,

then there are pretty
much no external torque
acting on her.
And so her total angular momentum
must stay the same as she pulls her arms
and leg closer,

which means this product
should stay the same,

which means as her
rotational inertia decreases,
her angular velocity should increase
to keep the product the same.
And that's why she speeds up.
Let's have a look now.
Here we go.
This is what's happening.
Isn't that amazing?
Wow.

And something very similar
happens to the stars.

A star usually forms
with huge swirling clouds
of gas gravitating together.
Now, if you consider this entire cloud

as our system, then
particles are distributed
very far away from the axis of rotation.
It's all spread out.
So it has a very high rotational inertia,
and it also has some angular velocity.
And so it has some angular momentum,
which is the product of these two.
But when eventually a star is formed,
look, almost all these particles

and our distributed
much closer than before.
So the rotational inertia
is much lower than before,

but the angular momentum
must stay the same
because pretty much no external torque
acts on our system, and therefore,
the product should stay the same.
And so the angular velocity
of the star must be much higher
than it was before,
such that the product stays the same.
That's why the star will be spinning
faster than before.
And when big stars run out of fuel
to sustain nuclear fusion at the core,
gravity crushes the whole thing
into a very tiny, very compact object
called neutron stars.
As a result, the rotational inertia
becomes incredibly low, but again,
the angular momentum must stay pretty
much the same as before, and therefore,

it's angular velocity
will be much, much higher
so that the product stays the same.
Now, this is not to scale,
just to give you some feeling for numbers.
Stars usually finish one rotation

in like a few days,
but these neutron stars
will rotate tens or even hundreds
of times per second.
So now you can see why.
It's all because of conservation
of angular momentum.
By the way, before we close, did you know
how we actually discovered these?
Some of them actually shoot out beams
of electromagnetic radiation
from their magnetic poles.
And as the star spins, those beams sweep
across the space like a cosmic lighthouse,
if you may.
And about 60 years ago,
astronomers noticed incredibly regular
radio pulses arriving from deep space.
It was about 1.3 seconds apart.
And so the signal was so precise

and it was so regular
that at first they thought
it couldn't be natural at all.

Some even wondered if
it might be intelligent
alien life signaling us.
So the mysterious source was labeled LGM-1

for little green men until
we found more of them,

and then finally realized
that they were actually
rapidly spinning neutron stars.
